Title: Gene cluster conservation provides insight into cercosporin biosynthesis and extends production to the genus Colletotrichum
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cercosporin facilitator protein; MFS transporter-like protein
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Photoactivated toxin contributing to virulence and requiring fungal autoresistance/handling
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for wild-type cercosporin production/autoresistance and included within the expanded CTB cluster; recognized here as part of the conserved cluster
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion mutant assayed by HPLC; comparative genomics; expression analysis under light vs dark
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Previously characterized as involved in cercosporin autoresistance; this study places CFP within the expanded evolutionarily conserved CTB cluster
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CFP is conserved adjacent to the CTB biosynthetic genes and should be considered part of the expanded CTB cluster. 2. CFP was previously shown to mediate cercosporin autoresistance/production and is light induced with CTB genes. 3. Its inclusion strengthens the view that the transferred cluster contains both biosynthetic and self-protection functions.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Targeting CFP could sensitize pathogens to their own toxin and interfere with toxin production; relevant for disease control rather than crop breeding
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Transport/self-protection disruption may alter fungal physiology beyond toxin production
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing/comparative genomics; expression analysis; HPLC mutant profiling

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Not required for cercosporin biosynthesis despite proximity to the CTB cluster
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion; HPLC metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Noted among flanking genes tested under light vs dark; induction context examined
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light versus dark condition used to assess cluster-associated expression
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Explicitly excluded from the functional CTB biosynthetic cluster because deletion mutants still produced cercosporin
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CBET3_00840 is a flanking PAL-like gene adjacent to the CTB cluster. 2. Although considered a candidate cluster gene based on synteny/expression context, deletion showed it is dispensable for cercosporin production. 3. Therefore it is not part of the core biosynthetic machinery.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Low priority target for manipulating cercosporin production because knockout did not alter production
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing/comparative genomics; expression analysis; metabolite profiling

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Not required for cercosporin biosynthesis despite flank conservation testing
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion; HPLC metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not induced under light in the same way as core 3' CTB extension genes
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light versus dark condition used to assess expression
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Excluded from the expanded core CTB biosynthetic cluster because deletion mutants retained normal cercosporin-like HPLC profile
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CBET3_00846 was evaluated as a candidate flanking CTB-associated gene. 2. Expression and knockout data indicated it is dispensable for cercosporin biosynthesis. 3. The functional CTB cluster therefore does not extend to this gene.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Low value target for altering cercosporin biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing/comparative genomics; expression analysis; metabolite profiling

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Synthesizes the polyketide precursor nor-toralactone as the first committed step of cercosporin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Photoactivated toxin that generates reactive oxygen species and contributes to fungal virulence on plants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for cercosporin biosynthesis; CTB1 expression marks cercosporin-producing conditions and is induced by TSA and during apple infection in Colletotrichum fioriniae
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics; prior knockout evidence referenced; qRT-PCR expression analysis in C. fioriniae; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: First committed step; supplies core scaffold precursor
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as first committed step in the pathway
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced under light in Cercospora beticola; in C. fioriniae induced by trichostatin A and detected during apple infection
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light induces cercosporin biosynthesis; darkness represses; trichostatin A induces production in C. fioriniae; in planta infection context
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Orthologs are widely conserved across Cercospora, Colletotrichum, Magnaporthe, and other fungi, consistent with duplication and horizontal transfer of the CTB cluster
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CTB1/CbCTB1 identified the cercosporin PKS cluster in C. beticola by strong collinearity with the C. nicotianae CTB cluster. 2. CTB1 ortholog phylogeny supported repeated duplication and horizontal transfer of the CTB cluster across fungi. 3. CTB1 is the first committed step of cercosporin biosynthesis and its expression correlates with cercosporin production, including TSA-induced production in C. fioriniae.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Targeting CTB1 would likely abolish cercosporin production and may reduce virulence of cercosporin-dependent pathogens; useful for pathogen diagnostics and antifungal intervention rather than crop breeding
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential effects on fungal fitness/virulence not fully quantified in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing and comparative genomics; qRT-PCR; genome assemblies under BioProject PRJNA270309

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes a late oxidative step required for conversion of precercosporin to cercosporin, likely participating in methylenedioxy bridge formation
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Precercosporin or closely related late intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Deletion abolishes cercosporin production and causes accumulation of precercosporin
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion; HPLC; UV-Vis; high-resolution MS; 1H/13C-NMR structural analysis of accumulated intermediate
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for direct late conversion of precursor to target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Late essential step, not explicitly designated rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous light induces expression and cercosporin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Provides evidence that the CTB cluster extends beyond the previously recognized eight genes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CTB9 is part of the expanded CTB cluster identified by conserved microsynteny and light induction. 2. CTB9 knockout abolished cercosporin and accumulated precercosporin. 3. CTB9 likely functions with CTB10 in installing the unusual seven-membered methylenedioxy bridge.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: An attractive antifungal target because disrupting CTB9 blocks cercosporin production at a late step and leads to unstable intermediate accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Accumulation of unstable intermediate precercosporin may affect interpretation of toxicity or fitness
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing/comparative genomics; expression analysis; metabolite profiling

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Functions in a late conversion step from precercosporin toward cercosporin, likely facilitating closure of the seven-membered methylenedioxy ring with CTB9
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Precercosporin or closely related late intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Deletion abolishes cercosporin production and causes accumulation of precercosporin
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion; HPLC; UV-Vis; high-resolution MS of accumulated intermediate
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for direct late conversion of precursor to target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Late essential step, not explicitly designated rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous light induces expression and cercosporin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Precise mechanistic role is unclear; authors suggest facilitation of unfavorable seven-membered ring closure
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CTB10 is an added CTB cluster gene identified by microsynteny and light-responsive expression. 2. CTB10 knockout abolished cercosporin and accumulated precercosporin. 3. CTB10 likely collaborates with CTB9 in methylenedioxy bridge formation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for suppressing cercosporin production in pathogens carrying the CTB cluster
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing/comparative genomics; expression analysis; metabolite profiling

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Required for cercosporin biosynthesis; authors hypothesize CTB11 with CTB12 may act early to dimerize the CTB3 product into the first perylenequinone intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Likely cercoquinone C or related intermediate (inferred)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion; HPLC metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for biosynthetic progression toward target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Essential step not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous light induces expression and cercosporin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function remains putative; the mechanistic role is inferred from gene family and knockout phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CTB11 belongs to the expanded CTB cluster defined by conserved microsynteny and light induction. 2. CTB11 deletion abolished cercosporin production. 3. Authors propose CTB11 and CTB12 act together early in the pathway to generate the first perylenequinone intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target for blocking toxin biosynthesis in multiple plant pathogens harboring CTB clusters
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing/comparative genomics; expression analysis; metabolite profiling

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Required for cercosporin biosynthesis; likely participates with CTB11 in oxidative coupling/dimerization to form an early perylenequinone intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion; HPLC metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for biosynthetic progression toward target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Essential step not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous light induces expression and cercosporin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The proposed oxidative dimerization role is inferred from laccase precedent and discussion, not directly biochemically demonstrated in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CTB12 is a newly defined CTB cluster member supported by microsynteny and expression. 2. CTB12 knockout abolished cercosporin production. 3. CTB12 is proposed to function with CTB11 in oxidative coupling early in perylenequinone assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to disable cercosporin formation in fungal pathogens
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mechanistic role inferred rather than directly assayed enzymatically
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing/comparative genomics; expression analysis; metabolite profiling

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional regulation of the CTB cercosporin biosynthetic cluster
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Photoactivated toxin contributing to plant pathogenesis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Previously implicated regulator of CTB cluster expression; in this paper highlighted as a conserved but comparatively divergent regulatory component of the CTB cluster
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Cluster-specific regulator implicated in control of cercosporin biosynthesis, but not functionally re-tested here
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Implicated activation of CTB biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Light-associated induction context for the CTB cluster
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics/phylogenomics; prior literature implication referenced
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Part of the light-responsive CTB cluster context
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light versus dark affects CTB cluster expression
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulatory target for suppressing cercosporin biosynthesis in pathogens, though direct validation in this study is limited
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Function in this paper is based mainly on prior implication; sequence divergence suggests possible species-specific regulatory evolution
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Sequence conservation of CTB8 is lower than other CTB proteins, suggesting positive diversifying selection
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CTB8 is the known Zn2Cys6 regulator embedded in the CTB cluster. 2. Comparative analyses showed CTB8 is less conserved than many biosynthetic CTB genes, suggesting diversifying selection. 3. It remains implicated as a transcriptional regulator of cercosporin biosynthesis but was not the main subject of new functional experiments here.
